name: Cova de sa Tortuga
description: Located near Cala Barcas, this cave is concealed by vegetation and requires navigating a tight entrance followed by a challenging traverse. Inside, a lake with crystal-clear waters is partially covered by a collapsed ceiling platform. The cave features notable speleothems showing subsidence-related displacement, abundant phreatic formations, and delicate soda-straw stalactites extending into the lake waters.
full_description:
Near the small cove of Cala Barcas, this cavity is found hidden among the vegetation. Access to the interior of the cavity is through an entrance of reduced dimensions, while the rest of the traverse will make us "suffer" for a few minutes until we reach the ramp of dirt and loose stones that will deposit us near the southern zone of the lake.

As we continue, leaving the lake water to our left, we can appreciate to our right a large group of formations in which subsidence processes that occurred in the cave can be observed, causing them to shift both vertically and horizontally. These processes may have occurred due to the dissolution of existing materials when being "attacked" by the brackish waters of the lake.

To continue the visit, one must climb to the right to reach a platform that is nothing other than the collapsed ceiling resulting from a collapse that occupied part of the lake. On said platform, in addition to stepping on the collapsed zone, there is an interesting variety of speleothems.

The lake holds crystal-clear waters, as well as some corners in the more remote areas where phreatic speleothems are abundant, not to mention the delicate speleothems popularly known as "macaroni" (soda straws), which on some occasions extend into the interior of the lake waters.
